world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: screen collapse revealing animated dragon with blinking eye (SNES/Master System shows dragon spitting fire)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: game end state triggered; victory sequence animation plays

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: tiles remain on board with no matching pairs accessible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: game ends in loss state; no further tile matching possible

environmental_storytelling.example: tile set contains traditional mahjong imagery: dots, bamboos, characters, winds (cardinal directions), dragons (red/green/white), seasons, and flowers—communicating cultural context and thematic richness through puzzle design
dragon character reveal functions as end-state signature; dragon's eye blinking (or fire-spitting variant) creates memorable visual closure and branded identity for the game series

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: animated sequence: eye blinks or spits fire; appears as screen collapses to reveal it

notes: Shanghai II is a mahjong solitaire puzzle game with minimal world-state communication. It lacks danger signals, faction systems, or political scenarios. Visual communication is restricted to tile matching mechanics and victory-state animation. The dragon serves as branded iconography rather than narrative agent. No enemy variants, faction colors, or environmental world-state shifts documented. Game focuses on abstract tile-matching rather than diegetic world representation.
